questions: Within the federal judicial system established under Article III, identify the tier of courts that sits directly below the nation’s highest court and is responsible for reviewing whether trial-level proceedings were fair and whether the law was applied correctly. Describe how these courts are structured geographically, how panels typically decide cases, the approximate scale of their annual caseload and how often their decisions are further reviewed. In addition, explain the research-driven supervision framework used by federal probation and pretrial officers who support the courts. In your answer, outline the core model that guides this framework, define its main principles, name the primary assessment tools used at different stages of a case, and summarize the key skills officers use to reduce recidivism.

Within the structure of the United States federal judiciary established under Article III, identify the intermediate appellate courts that review decisions from trial courts. Explain the standard process by which a losing party seeks review in these courts, including how arguments are presented, how decisions may become final, and under what circumstances further review by the nation’s highest court may occur. Additionally, name and describe the twice-yearly statistical publication that reports on the workload of these appellate courts (as well as trial and bankruptcy courts), specifying what time periods it covers, what kind of data it includes, and how it compares current caseload data to prior years.

Within the federal judicial system established under Article III, identify the level of court that primarily reviews decisions from trial courts to determine whether proceedings were fair and the law was applied correctly. Then, explain in detail how the appeals process functions at that level, including how cases are argued, how decisions are typically finalized, and what options exist for further review. Additionally, identify the recurring statistical report that analyzes the workload of these courts and related federal courts, specifying the time period it covers, the types of courts included in its analysis, and how far back its publicly available publications extend.

Within the structure of the U.S. federal judiciary established under Article III, appellate courts play a central role in reviewing lower court decisions. Identify all of the major recurring statistical publications issued by the federal judiciary that report on the workload of the U.S. courts of appeals (along with other federal courts), and for each publication explain: (1) the time period it covers and how that period is defined (e.g., ending date), (2) how frequently it is issued, and (3) the scope of courts or judicial activities it includes. In addition, provide a detailed explanation of how a typical appeal proceeds in the federal system, including the entitlement to appeal, the role of written briefs, the structure and purpose of oral argument (including typical time limits), how decisions are made (panel composition and the possibility of rehearing by a larger group of judges), the circumstances under which the Supreme Court may review a case, and key differences in how civil, criminal, and bankruptcy appeals are handled.

Within the judicial branch established by Article III of the U.S. Constitution, identify the tier of federal courts that sit directly below the nation’s highest court and are responsible for reviewing whether trial-level proceedings correctly applied the law. Describe their overall structure, including how many exist, how they are geographically organized, how cases are typically decided, the scope of their jurisdiction (including any court with nationwide specialized jurisdiction), and the extent to which their decisions are usually final. Additionally, explain the research-driven framework used by federal officers who supervise individuals before trial and after conviction, detailing the model that guides supervision, its three core principles, and the specific assessment tools used to evaluate risk at different stages.

logic_chain: About the U.S. Courts of Appeals
rounds.question: Within the federal judicial system established under Article III, identify the tier of courts that sits directly below the nation’s highest court and is responsible for reviewing whether trial-level proceedings were fair and whether the law was applied correctly. Describe how these courts are structured geographically, how panels typically decide cases, the approximate scale of their annual caseload and how often their decisions are further reviewed. In addition, explain the research-driven supervision framework used by federal probation and pretrial officers who support the courts. In your answer, outline the core model that guides this framework, define its main principles, name the primary assessment tools used at different stages of a case, and summarize the key skills officers use to reduce recidivism.
checklist_width: Explains that the courts review district (trial) court and some administrative agency decisions for correct application of law and fairness
States that there are 12 regional circuits plus a 13th court with nationwide specialized jurisdiction
Notes that cases are typically decided by panels of three judges and that these courts handle over 50,000 cases annually
Mentions that only a small percentage (about 10% or fewer) are appealed to the Supreme Court and that most decisions are final within their circuits
Identifies the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) Model as the foundation of supervision
Defines the Risk, Need, and Responsivity principles
Names the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA) and Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) tools
Describes core correctional practices such as relationship building, targeting criminal thinking, reinforcement/disapproval, and skill development to reduce recidivism
checklist_depth: U.S. Courts of Appeals (federal appellate courts below the U.S. Supreme Court) + Logic proof: they review district court decisions for fairness and correct application of law
Evidence-Based Practices in the federal probation and pretrial services system + Logic proof: structured around the Risk-Need-Responsivity Model using validated risk assessment tools (PTRA and PCRA)
rounds.rationale: The question uses structural logic from the federal court hierarchy (courts below the Supreme Court that review trial decisions) to indirectly point to the U.S. Courts of Appeals without naming them (Deep). It then requires aggregation of detailed structural, statistical, and procedural facts about those courts, plus a comprehensive explanation of the evidence-based supervision framework used by probation and pretrial officers (Wide), forcing synthesis across two distinct but related components of the federal judicial system.
source_nodes: About the U.S. Courts of Appeals
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: About the U.S. Courts of Appeals On this page Courts of appeals review challenges to court decisions to determine whether the proceedings were fair and the law was applied cor
rounds.answer_a:
The tier of courts directly below the Supreme Court is the U.S. Courts of Appeals (federal circuit courts). They review district court and some agency decisions to determine whether trials were fair and the law correctly applied, without re-trying facts. Geographically, there are 12 regional circuits plus the Federal Circuit; each covers a defined group of districts, with the Federal Circuit organized by subject matter rather than territory.[1][2] Cases are typically decided by three-judge panels, though all active judges in a circuit may sit en banc for especially important matters.[1] The courts of appeals receive roughly 40,000–45,000 new filings per year, while the Supreme Court grants plenary review in only about 60–70 cases annually, meaning only a tiny fraction of circuit decisions are further reviewed.[2][3]

Federal probation and pretrial services use a research-driven “Risk-Need-Responsivity” (RNR) framework.[4] Its core principles are: (1) Risk: match supervision intensity to risk of reoffending; (2) Need: target criminogenic needs (e.g., antisocial peers, substance abuse); (3) Responsivity: tailor interventions to individual characteristics (learning style, culture, motivation).[4][5] Primary assessment tools include the Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) for sentenced persons and the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA) at the pretrial stage.[4][6] Officers employ skills such as structured risk assessment, motivational interviewing, cognitive-behavioral techniques, problem-solving, and effective use of authority and incentives/sanctions to support behavior change and reduce recidivism.[4][5]

## References
[1] U.S. Courts – “Courts of Appeals”  
[2] U.S. Courts – “Understanding the Federal Courts”  
[3] Supreme Court of the United States – “About the Court” / Case Selection  
[4] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts – “Evidence-Based Practices” / RNR model  
[5] AOUSC – “Core Principles of Effective Intervention”  
[6] AOUSC – “Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA)” and “Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA)”
rounds.answer_b:
Within the Article III federal judicial system, the United States Courts of Appeals (Circuit Courts) sit directly below the Supreme Court, reviewing whether trial courts applied the law correctly and conducted fair proceedings [1]. Geographically, the system is divided into 13 appellate courts: 11 regional circuits, the District of Columbia Circuit, and the Federal Circuit [1]. Rather than utilizing juries, these courts typically decide cases using randomly assigned panels of three judges [2]. Annually, the appellate courts handle a massive caseload of roughly 40,000 to 50,000 cases [2]. Because the Supreme Court accepts fewer than two percent of cases appealed to it, the Courts of Appeals serve as the final judicial authority in the vast majority of federal litigation [1][2].

To support the federal courts, federal probation and pretrial officers utilize a research-driven, evidence-based supervision framework grounded in the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) core model [3]. The RNR model's main principles dictate matching the intensity of supervision to an individual's likelihood of reoffending (Risk), targeting the specific criminogenic factors driving their criminal behavior (Need), and tailoring interventions to their unique learning styles, motivations, and abilities (Responsivity) [3][4]. At the pretrial stage, officers administer the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA) tool, whereas the Post-Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) tool is used for individuals serving terms of probation or supervised release [4]. To actively reduce recidivism, officers apply specialized interactional skills cultivated through programs like STARR (Staff Training Aimed at Reducing Re-Arrest) [3]. These key skills include active listening, motivational interviewing, cognitive-behavioral techniques, and structured role-playing, which collaboratively guide positive behavioral change and promote long-term community safety [3][4].

## References
[1] United States Courts. "Court Role and Structure." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-role-and-structure
[2] United States Courts. "Appellate Courts and Cases – Journalist’s Guide." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/appellate-courts-and-cases-journalists-guide
[3] United States Courts. "Probation and Pretrial Services - Supervision." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/supervision
[4] United States Courts. "Chapter 1: Overview of EBP and the PCRA." U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services. https://www.uscourts.gov/sites/default/files/pcra_scoring_guide_edition_2_1.pdf
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify the U.S. Courts of Appeals as the tier below the Supreme Court and accurately explain their role in reviewing district court decisions for fairness and correct application of law. Both also correctly ground the supervision framework in the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model with appropriate tools (PTRA, PCRA), so both pass the core logic requirement.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both cover geographic structure (12 regional circuits plus D.C./Federal Circuit equivalent), three-judge panels, large annual caseload (~40,000–50,000), and the small percentage reviewed by the Supreme Court. Both define the RNR principles and list core supervision skills. Agent A slightly exceeds B in completeness by explicitly noting en banc review and mentioning review of administrative agency decisions. Citation density is also higher in A.

Finally, Presentation & User Experience: Both are clear and well-structured, but Agent A provides marginally richer contextual detail (e.g., en banc process, agency review, incentives/sanctions) and slightly more comprehensive sourcing. There are no major factual failures by either agent. Thus, A wins on overall depth and completeness rather than correctness alone.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Appeals The losing party in a decision by a trial court in the federal courts normally is entitled to appeal the decision to a federal court of appeals. The Process Although s
rounds.answer_a:
The intermediate appellate courts in the Article III federal judiciary are the U.S. Courts of Appeals (the federal circuit courts). They review final decisions and certain interlocutory orders from the U.S. District Courts, as well as some administrative agency decisions, to determine whether legal errors occurred; they generally accept the trial court’s factual findings unless clearly erroneous.[1][2]

A losing party (the appellant) usually begins review by filing a notice of appeal in the district court within a set deadline (commonly 30 days in civil cases, shorter in many criminal cases).[1] The appellant then files a written brief arguing legal error; the appellee responds, and sometimes a reply brief follows.[1][2] Many cases are decided on the briefs alone, but courts often schedule oral argument, where counsel present condensed arguments and answer questions from a three‑judge panel.[1]

After deliberation, the panel issues a written opinion or an unpublished order. If no further review is sought, or if rehearing is denied, the court issues its mandate and the judgment becomes final in the lower courts.[1][2] A party may request panel rehearing or rehearing en banc (before all active judges in the circuit) for questions of exceptional importance or to maintain intra‑circuit uniformity.[1]

Further review by the nation’s highest court, the U.S. Supreme Court, is sought through a petition for writ of certiorari. The Court has almost complete discretion and grants review in a very small fraction of cases, typically those involving conflicts among circuits or important federal questions.[2][3]

Workload for the courts of appeals, district courts, and bankruptcy courts is reported twice a year in the Judicial Business of the United States Courts and the companion Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary.[4] These cover the 12‑month periods ending March 31 and September 30 (fiscal year), and include data on filings, terminations, and pending cases by court, case type, and nature of suit, with tables comparing current totals and rates to prior years to show trends in caseload volume and composition.[4][5]

## References
[1] U.S. Courts – “Courts of Appeals.”  
[2] U.S. Courts – “Appeals Courts: A Brief Overview” / “Understanding the Federal Courts.”  
[3] Supreme Court of the United States – “Case Selection and Review.”  
[4] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts – “Judicial Business of the United States Courts.”  
[5] AOUSC – “Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary.”
rounds.answer_b:
Within the Article III federal judicial system, the intermediate appellate courts are the United States Courts of Appeals, frequently referred to as Circuit Courts [1]. When a losing party in a federal trial court seeks review, they typically exercise their right to appeal by submitting written briefs to the appellate court [2]. The appellant's brief argues that the trial court committed legal or procedural errors, while the appellee's brief defends the trial court's original decision [2]. Cases are usually decided by a randomly assigned panel of three federal appellate judges [2]. While some cases involve oral arguments where lawyers present their points directly to the judges, many claims are resolved entirely based on the submitted written briefs [1][2]. 

These panel decisions generally become final unless the circuit court agrees to rehear the case *en banc* (involving all active judges of the circuit) or the losing party successfully petitions the United States Supreme Court for a writ of certiorari [2]. The Supreme Court is not obligated to hear appeals; it grants further review in a tiny fraction of cases, primarily under compelling circumstances such as resolving conflicting interpretations of federal law among different circuits, deciding major constitutional questions, or addressing issues of broad national significance [1][2].

To monitor the operations of this expansive system, the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts produces a twice-yearly statistical publication titled *Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary* [3]. This publication is released for the 12-month periods ending on June 30 and December 31 [3]. It provides comprehensive workload data outlining the volume of case filings, terminations, and pending actions across the federal appellate, district, and bankruptcy courts, alongside operational figures for the probation and pretrial services systems [3]. To contextualize these figures and identify systemic trends, the report systematically compares current caseload data to data from the identical 12-month reporting periods in previous years [3].

## References
[1] United States Courts. "Court Role and Structure." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-role-and-structure
[2] United States Courts. "Appellate Courts and Cases – Journalist’s Guide." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/appellate-courts-and-cases-journalists-guide
[3] United States Courts. "Caseload Statistics Data Tables." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/caseload-statistics-data-tables
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identify the U.S. Courts of Appeals as the intermediate appellate courts (passes DEEP logic for both). Both also accurately describe the appeals process (briefs, three-judge panel, possible oral argument, rehearing, certiorari as discretionary). However, Agent A contains a significant factual error: it states the statistical publication covers 12-month periods ending March 31 and September 30, which is incorrect (it should be June 30 and December 31). This violates the WIDTH checklist. Agent B correctly identifies the publication timing and its scope (including probation and pretrial services) and accurately describes comparative caseload reporting, though it omits the specific reference to the Judicial Caseload Indicators table comparing 1-, 5-, and 10-year data. Because Agent A includes a clear factual mistake while Agent B is largely complete and accurate, Agent B provides the better overall user experience. The failure is WIDE (detail-level factual inaccuracy), not DEEP.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Appeals The losing party in a decision by a trial court in the federal courts normally is entitled to appeal the decision to a federal court of appeals. The Process Although s
rounds.answer_a:
The level of court that primarily reviews decisions from federal trial courts for fairness and correct application of law is the U.S. Courts of Appeals (the federal circuit courts), the intermediate appellate courts created under Article III.[1]

An appeal typically begins when the losing party (the appellant) files a notice of appeal in the district court within the prescribed time (usually 30 days in civil cases, shorter in many criminal cases).[1][2] The record on appeal—transcripts, filings, and exhibits—is transmitted to the court of appeals. The appellant submits an opening brief arguing that the district court made legal or procedural errors; the appellee files an answering brief; the appellant may file a reply.[1]

Most appeals are decided by a three‑judge panel. Many cases are resolved on the written briefs alone; in others, the panel holds oral argument, during which counsel present condensed arguments (often 10–20 minutes per side) and respond to judges’ questions.[1][2] After deliberation, the panel issues a written opinion or a shorter, unpublished disposition. Unless a party timely seeks rehearing, the court issues its mandate, returning jurisdiction to the district court and making the appellate judgment final in that system.[1]

Parties may petition for panel rehearing or rehearing en banc (before all active circuit judges or a larger en banc panel) in cases presenting questions of exceptional importance or conflicts within the circuit.[1] After final action in the court of appeals, a party may seek further review in the U.S. Supreme Court by filing a petition for a writ of certiorari. The Supreme Court has almost complete discretion and grants review in a very small fraction of cases, focusing on important federal questions and conflicts among circuits.[2][3]

The recurring statistical report that analyzes the workload of courts of appeals and related federal courts is the Judicial Business of the United States Courts, accompanied by the Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary.[4] It is issued annually for the 12‑month period ending September 30 (the federal judiciary’s fiscal year) and covers courts of appeals, district courts, and bankruptcy courts, among others.[4][5] The Administrative Office makes these annual reports publicly available back to at least the mid‑1990s online, with some earlier volumes accessible in archival form.[4][5]

## References
[1] U.S. Courts – “Courts of Appeals.”  
[2] U.S. Courts – “Appeals Courts: A Brief Overview” / “Understanding the Federal Courts.”  
[3] Supreme Court of the United States – “Case Selection and Review.”  
[4] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts – “Judicial Business of the United States Courts.”  
[5] AOUSC – “Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary” / report archives.
rounds.answer_b:
Within the Article III federal judicial system, the United States Courts of Appeals—commonly known as Circuit Courts—are the intermediate appellate level responsible for reviewing decisions from trial courts to ensure proceedings were fair and the law was applied correctly [1].

The appellate process typically begins when a losing party files a notice of appeal and submits written briefs [2]. The appellant’s brief identifies specific legal or procedural errors made by the trial court, while the appellee’s brief defends the trial court’s ruling [2]. Unlike trial courts, appellate courts do not use juries or collect new evidence. Instead, appeals are standardly decided by a randomly assigned panel of three federal judges [2]. Many cases are resolved entirely based on the submitted written briefs and the established case record. However, in certain complex cases, the panel may schedule oral arguments where attorneys present their points directly to the judges and answer their questions dynamically [1][2]. 

Once the three-judge panel issues its ruling, the decision generally becomes final [2]. If a party is dissatisfied with the outcome, two primary avenues for further review exist. First, the losing party can request a rehearing *en banc*, asking all active judges of that specific circuit to reconsider the case [2]. Second, the party may petition the United States Supreme Court for a writ of certiorari [1]. The Supreme Court is not required to take these appeals and grants review in only a small fraction of cases, typically prioritizing disputes involving significant constitutional questions or conflicting legal interpretations among different circuits [1][2].

To analyze the immense workload of these institutions, the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts publishes the *Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary* [3]. This statistical report is released twice annually, covering twelve-month reporting periods that end on June 30 and December 31 [3]. The publication comprehensively tracks caseload data—such as filings, terminations, and pending actions—for the U.S. Courts of Appeals, U.S. District Courts, and U.S. Bankruptcy Courts, while also detailing operations for federal probation and pretrial services [3]. On the federal judiciary’s official website, the publicly available archives for these comprehensive statistical publications extend back to the report ending December 31, 2000 [3].

## References
[1] United States Courts. "Court Role and Structure." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-role-and-structure
[2] United States Courts. "Appellate Courts and Cases – Journalist’s Guide." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/appellate-courts-and-cases-journalists-guide
[3] United States Courts. "Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/caseload-statistics-data-tables
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the U.S. Courts of Appeals as the intermediate appellate courts reviewing district court decisions, and both recognized the recurring workload publication (though misnamed). So both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both described the appellate process well (three-judge panels, written briefs, oral argument, en banc rehearing, certiorari). However, Agent A explicitly included typical oral argument time allotments (10–20 minutes), satisfying a checklist requirement that Agent B omitted. On the statistical report, both agents failed a key checklist item: neither identified the Federal Judicial Caseload Statistics covering the 12-month period ending March 31. Agent A incorrectly stated the period ends September 30; Agent B incorrectly stated it is issued twice annually ending June 30 and December 31. Both therefore have significant WIDE errors. Agent B also omitted the March 31 period and misstated publication frequency, which is a more substantial deviation.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent A provides slightly richer procedural detail (mandate issuance, unpublished dispositions, time allotments) and comparable structure. Formatting is similar for both; neither uses strong markdown structuring, but both are readable. Agent A’s explanation is marginally more comprehensive and nuanced.

Conclusion: Since both have factual errors regarding the statistical report, neither qualifies for MUCH_BETTER. However, Agent A is more complete on appellate procedure and slightly less incorrect overall. Therefore, Agent A wins on breadth and detail despite shared inaccuracies.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

Judicial Business of the United States Courts
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Appeals The losing party in a decision by a trial court in the federal courts normally is entitled to appeal the decision to a federal court of appeals. The Process Although s
rounds.answer_a:
The principal recurring federal judiciary statistical publications that report on courts of appeals workload are:

1. **Judicial Business of the United States Courts (Annual Report)** – Covers the 12‑month period ending September 30 (the federal judiciary’s fiscal year).[1] Issued once per year by the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts (AO). It provides narrative and tables on filings, terminations, and pending cases in the U.S. courts of appeals, district courts, and bankruptcy courts, as well as probation and pretrial services and other Article III and Article I courts.[1][2]

2. **Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary** – Published in tandem with Judicial Business for the same 12‑month period ending September 30, annually.[1][2] It consists of detailed tables (without lengthy narrative) for courts of appeals, district courts, bankruptcy courts, the U.S. Court of International Trade, the U.S. Court of Federal Claims, and federal probation/pretrial.[2] Online tables are available back to at least the mid‑1990s, with older data in earlier printed volumes.[1][2]

3. **Judicial Caseload Indicators / Federal Court Management Statistics** – Annual, using the same fiscal‑year period ending September 30, presenting high‑level indicators (filings per judgeship, median time to disposition, etc.) for each court of appeals and district court.[2]

**Appeals process**

Most final judgments in federal civil and criminal cases are appealable once as of right to a U.S. court of appeals; some interlocutory orders and certain bankruptcy matters are also appealable under statute.[3] The appellant files a notice of appeal in the trial court, the record is prepared, and the parties file written briefs—primary vehicles for legal argument.[3]

Appeals are typically decided by a three‑judge panel.[3] Many cases are submitted on the briefs; others receive oral argument, usually 10–20 minutes per side, focused on judges’ questions about legal issues and the record.[3] After conference, the panel issues a written opinion or summary disposition. A party may seek panel rehearing or rehearing en banc (by all active judges or a larger en banc panel) in cases of intra‑circuit conflict or exceptional importance.[3]

Civil and criminal appeals follow similar procedures, but criminal appeals often have expedited schedules and distinct standards (e.g., plain‑error review, sentencing issues).[3] Bankruptcy appeals may go to a district court or Bankruptcy Appellate Panel before the court of appeals.[3][4] Further review in the Supreme Court is by petition for writ of certiorari; review is discretionary and granted in a small fraction of cases involving important federal questions or circuit splits.[5]

## References
[1] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts, “Judicial Business of the United States Courts,” overview and archives.  
[2] AOUSC, “Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary” and “Federal Court Management Statistics / Judicial Caseload Indicators.”  
[3] U.S. Courts, “Understanding the Federal Courts” and “Courts of Appeals” pages.  
[4] U.S. Courts, “Bankruptcy Appeals” (district courts and Bankruptcy Appellate Panels).  
[5] Supreme Court of the United States, “Case Selection and Review” / “FAQs – The Court and Its Procedures.”
rounds.answer_b:
The U.S. federal judiciary issues three primary recurring statistical publications that track the workload of the U.S. Courts of Appeals alongside other federal legal bodies. First, the *Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary* is issued twice annually, covering 12-month periods ending June 30 and December 31. It details case filings, terminations, and pending actions for appellate, district, and bankruptcy courts, plus probation and pretrial services [1]. Second, *Judicial Business of the United States Courts* is published annually for the federal fiscal year ending September 30, providing a comprehensive analysis of the workload across all appellate, district, and bankruptcy courts [2]. Third, *Federal Court Management Statistics* is issued quarterly (for 12-month periods ending March 31, June 30, September 30, and December 31). It provides court-by-court statistical profiles strictly for appellate and district courts to contextualize operational backlogs and processing times [3].

In the federal system, a losing trial party generally has a statutory right to appeal an adverse ruling [4]. The process relies heavily on written briefs: the appellant's brief argues the trial court committed legal or procedural errors, while the appellee's brief defends the original ruling [4]. Cases are decided by randomly assigned three-judge panels without juries or new evidence [4]. Although many disputes are resolved solely via briefs, courts schedule oral arguments for complex cases. During oral argument, lawyers present their case and answer judges' questions dynamically, with time strictly limited—usually to just 10 or 15 minutes per side [4]. 

Panel decisions are final unless a party successfully petitions for a rehearing *en banc* (involving all active judges in that circuit) or the U.S. Supreme Court grants a writ of certiorari [4]. The Supreme Court typically accepts cases only to resolve conflicting circuit interpretations or adjudicate major constitutional questions [4].

Appeal entitlements differ structurally by case type. In civil litigation, either plaintiff or defendant may appeal an unfavorable judgment [4]. In criminal cases, defendants may appeal their convictions or sentences, but constitutional Double Jeopardy protections prohibit the government from appealing a "not guilty" verdict [4]. Bankruptcy appeals possess a unique two-tiered structure: they are first appealed to a U.S. District Court or a Bankruptcy Appellate Panel (BAP), and only subsequently can they be appealed to the U.S. Courts of Appeals [4].

## References
[1] United States Courts. "Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/caseload-statistics-data-tables 
[2] United States Courts. "Judicial Business." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/analysis-reports/judicial-business-united-states-courts
[3] United States Courts. "Federal Court Management Statistics." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/analysis-reports/federal-court-management-statistics
[4] United States Courts. "Appellate Courts and Cases – Journalist’s Guide." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/appellate-courts-and-cases-journalists-guide
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified Judicial Business of the United States Courts and generally explained the appellate process well (entitlement to appeal, briefs, three-judge panels, en banc rehearing, certiorari, bankruptcy path, and criminal double jeopardy limits). However, both fail Deep Logic and Width requirements on the statistical publications. Agent A incorrectly states that the Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary are annual and tied to the September 30 fiscal year (they are issued twice yearly for 12-month periods ending June 30 and December 31). Agent B correctly describes Statistical Tables but misidentifies the third major publication, substituting Federal Court Management Statistics (quarterly) instead of Federal Judicial Caseload Statistics (annual, 12-month period ending March 31, with 1-, 5-, and 10-year comparative indicators). Neither properly captures all required reporting periods and comparative features for Publication 3. Because both contain significant factual errors in core publication details (DEEP) and omit required checklist elements (WIDE), this is a Low-Quality Tie despite otherwise strong appellate-process explanations and decent formatting.

logic_chain: About the U.S. Courts of Appeals
rounds.question: Within the judicial branch established by Article III of the U.S. Constitution, identify the tier of federal courts that sit directly below the nation’s highest court and are responsible for reviewing whether trial-level proceedings correctly applied the law. Describe their overall structure, including how many exist, how they are geographically organized, how cases are typically decided, the scope of their jurisdiction (including any court with nationwide specialized jurisdiction), and the extent to which their decisions are usually final. Additionally, explain the research-driven framework used by federal officers who supervise individuals before trial and after conviction, detailing the model that guides supervision, its three core principles, and the specific assessment tools used to evaluate risk at different stages.
checklist_width: Total number of intermediate appellate courts (12 regional circuits plus 1 specialized nationwide court)
Explanation that they review district court decisions for correct application of law and fairness, without retrying cases or hearing new evidence
Description of three-judge panels and absence of juries at this level
Note that most of their decisions are final, with only a small percentage reviewed by the Supreme Court
Identification of the specialized court with nationwide jurisdiction and examples of its subject matter (e.g., patent law, certain trade or federal claims cases)
Name and explanation of the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) Model
Description of the Risk Principle
Description of the Need Principle
Description of the Responsivity Principle
Identification and purpose of the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA)
Identification and purpose of the Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA)
Reference to core correctional practices used to reduce recidivism
checklist_depth: U.S. Courts of Appeals (13 total: 12 regional circuits plus the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit) + Proof that they review trial court decisions for correct application of law
Federal probation and pretrial services officers using the Evidence-Based Practices framework grounded in the Risk-Need-Responsivity Model + Proof through mention of PTRA and PCRA tools
rounds.rationale: The question uses constitutional structure (Article III hierarchy and review function) to indirectly identify the U.S. Courts of Appeals without naming them, requiring logical recognition of their role (Deep). It also requires aggregation of multiple structural and statistical details about these courts. In parallel, it asks for a comprehensive explanation of the Evidence-Based Practices framework used by federal supervision officers, including its principles and tools, forcing the respondent to synthesize multiple components (Wide). Together, this creates a multi-entity, multi-layered search task within the domain of Court Role and Structure.
source_nodes: About the U.S. Courts of Appeals
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: About the U.S. Courts of Appeals On this page Courts of appeals review challenges to court decisions to determine whether the proceedings were fair and the law was applied cor
rounds.answer_a:
The tier of courts directly below the U.S. Supreme Court in the Article III federal judiciary is the U.S. Courts of Appeals (often called the federal circuit courts). They review district court and certain federal agency decisions to determine whether the law was applied correctly and whether proceedings were legally fair, without conducting new trials.[1][2]

There are 13 courts of appeals: 12 regional circuits (First through Eleventh Circuits plus the D.C. Circuit), each covering a defined group of federal district courts, and the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit, which has nationwide subject‑matter jurisdiction rather than a geographic region.[1][3] The Federal Circuit hears specialized appeals, including patent cases and many claims against the federal government.[3]

Appeals are normally decided by three‑judge panels, drawn from the circuit’s active judges (and sometimes senior judges). In especially important or complex cases, all active judges in a circuit may rehear a case en banc.[1][2] These courts handle tens of thousands of appeals annually and, because the Supreme Court grants plenary review in only a small number of cases each term, their decisions are usually the final word in federal litigation.[2][4]

Federal probation and pretrial services officers use a research‑driven Risk‑Need‑Responsivity (RNR) framework to supervise individuals both pretrial and post‑conviction.[5] The model rests on three principles:  
• Risk: match the intensity of supervision and services to the person’s risk of reoffending.[5]  
• Need: focus on “criminogenic” needs—dynamic factors like antisocial attitudes, substance abuse, and unstable employment that are linked to crime.[5]  
• Responsivity: tailor interventions to individual characteristics (e.g., learning style, culture, motivation, mental health) to maximize behavior change.[5][6]

Key risk tools include the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA) for people awaiting trial and the Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) for those on probation or supervised release.[5][6] Officers combine these tools with skills such as motivational interviewing and cognitive‑behavioral techniques to reduce recidivism.[5][6]

## References
[1] U.S. Courts – “Courts of Appeals”  
[2] U.S. Courts – “Understanding the Federal Courts”  
[3] U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit – Jurisdiction Overview  
[4] Supreme Court of the U.S. – “Case Selection Process”  
[5] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts – “Evidence‑Based Practices in Federal Supervision”  
[6] AOUSC – “Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA)” and “Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA)”
rounds.answer_b:
Within the judicial branch established by Article III, the United States Courts of Appeals sit directly below the Supreme Court. These appellate courts are responsible for reviewing whether trial-level courts applied the law correctly and conducted fair proceedings [1]. In total, there are 13 appellate courts. Twelve of these are geographically based regional circuits, which include 11 numbered circuits spanning various states and one for the District of Columbia [1]. The thirteenth is the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit, which has nationwide jurisdiction over specialized cases, such as patent disputes and international trade [1]. Cases in the appellate courts are typically decided by randomly assigned panels of three judges rather than by juries [2]. Because the Supreme Court accepts fewer than two percent of the appeals filed each year, decisions made by the Courts of Appeals are overwhelmingly final, making them the last judicial authority in the vast majority of federal litigation [2].

To supervise individuals before trial and after conviction, federal probation and pretrial services officers employ a research-driven, evidence-based practices framework centered on the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model [3]. The RNR model guides supervision through three core principles. First, the Risk principle dictates that supervision intensity must correspond to an individual's statistical likelihood of reoffending, preserving intensive resources for higher-risk individuals [4]. Second, the Need principle requires officers to target specific criminogenic factors—such as substance abuse or criminal associates—that directly influence criminal behavior [4]. Third, the Responsivity principle involves tailoring cognitive-behavioral interventions to a person's unique learning style, motivation, and abilities to maximize the effectiveness of rehabilitation [4].

To operationalize this framework accurately, officers utilize specialized assessment tools at different judicial stages to evaluate risk objectively. At the initial pretrial stage, officers administer the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA) to evaluate the likelihood that a defendant will fail to appear in court or will commit a new crime before trial [3]. During the post-conviction phase, such as probation or supervised release, officers administer the Post-Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) to identify distinct risks and tailor dynamic treatment plans aimed at reducing long-term recidivism [4].

## References
[1] United States Courts. "Court Role and Structure." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-role-and-structure
[2] United States Courts. "Appellate Courts and Cases – Journalist’s Guide." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/appellate-courts-and-cases-journalists-guide
[3] United States Courts. "Probation and Pretrial Services - Supervision." Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/supervision
[4] United States Courts. "Chapter 1: Overview of EBP and the PCRA." U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services. https://www.uscourts.gov/sites/default/files/pcra_scoring_guide_edition_2_1.pdf
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify the U.S. Courts of Appeals (13 total: 12 regional circuits plus the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit) and accurately explain that they review trial court decisions for correct application of law without retrying cases. Both also correctly describe the Evidence-Based Practices framework grounded in the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) Model and explicitly reference the PTRA and PCRA tools. Thus, both pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both responses cover all major checklist items—structure (13 courts, geographic organization), three-judge panels and absence of juries, limited Supreme Court review, Federal Circuit’s nationwide specialized jurisdiction (e.g., patents), RNR principles (Risk, Need, Responsivity), PTRA and PCRA purposes, and reference to cognitive-behavioral/core correctional practices. Agent A slightly exceeds in completeness by mentioning en banc review and explicitly stating that no new evidence is heard, adding useful procedural clarity.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: While both are clear and well-organized, Agent A uses more scannable formatting (bullet points for RNR principles, clearer sectional breaks) and provides slightly richer contextual detail. It also includes a higher citation count, reinforcing groundedness. Agent B is accurate but more text-dense and less visually structured.

Because both are factually strong and complete, but Agent A offers marginally better formatting, scannability, and contextual depth, Agent A is the better overall user experience. There are no substantive failures from Agent B.
